channel.title: +MT on Vishvas's notes
channel.description: Recent content in +MT on Vishvas's notes

item.description: The glorious MACS J1149.5+2223 cluster with 100s of galaxies visualized by JWST. When the light from this cluster at 5 billion light-years away started on its journey towards us the sun and solar system were not yet born. How many types of life and civilization have emerged and vanished among these 100s of galaxies dominated by a central elliptical will probably remain entirely unknown to us. The very existence of this cluster was unknown till the 1990s, and about 3-4 decades later, we have this stupendously clear image of it. One needs to repeatedly gaze at such images, as it simultaneously makes existence feel utterly meaningless or so deeply meaningful that we can barely grasp it.

item.title: Artemis, sun sibling, aliens
item.description:
Source: TW

The Artemis flight is sobering in a way. This is the farthest a manned flight has ever gone. That is a big deal, but everything else in the hostile solar system is so much farther away; what to say of the Alpha Centauri system we are passing by and then the rest of the Milky Way?

To put it in perspective and to show how much time matters: The sun was not close to Alpha Centauri or Sirius. We are just passing by them. The sun was born far from its current location in a cluster close to its sibling HD 162826, which is now a star 110 light years away in the constellation of Hercules. That’s the distance they have drifted since their birth. It is a slightly brighter and bigger star; hence, if it has any planets around it, they would have to be farther than the Earth in orbit to support life. Right now, we don’t have a clue if it has a planet or not.

item.title: Cut appalachia
item.description: A mahAmlechCha outdoorsman mentioned wanting to spend a part of his life walking the “International Appalachian Trail”: It is staggering when we contemplate that the Appalachians, the North African Atlas mountains, the Irish and Scottish Highlands, the Highlands of Greenland and the mountains of Norway are all the same mountain range rent asunder as if by the vajra of vR^itrahan

item.title: Kant - disks
item.description:
Source: TW

Several proto-planetary discs imaged by the ALMA interferometer. It is remarkable that the German philosopher Immanuel Kant had come up with a theory of the origin of solar systems that broadly laid out the foundations of the modern theory of planetary origins in 1755 CE.

Kant had none of the modern knowledge of our own and other solar systems. Yet he connected to something deep (inspired by the ancient Roman philosopher Lucretius Rarus) when he correctly posited the formation of structures like the protoplanetary discs directly imaged here by ALMA and the formation of planets within them. This and his thoughts on natural laws lead us to regard Kant highly among the neo-Occidental philosophers.

item.title: Metallicity
item.description:
Source: TW

The general theory to date has been that the universe needs time before it can start forming planets. The earliest stars have low metallicity, i.e., elements higher than H and He. As per the current models, only when these higher elements emerge can they form dust and organic compounds, and the dust can coalesce to form planets and life.

In the early galaxies, the paucity of carbon and the intense, unshielded UV radiation from young stars were believed to prevent the formation of complex molecules.
